seo.title: Jamaica Payroll Tax Rates 2026: Complete Guide to PAYE, NIS, NHT & Education Tax
seo.description: Full breakdown of Jamaica's 2026 statutory payroll rates: PAYE threshold, NIS 3%, NHT 2%/3%, Education Tax 2.25%/3.5%. Includes tables and worked examples.

title: Jamaica Payroll Tax Rates 2026: Complete Guide to PAYE, NIS, NHT & Education Tax
subtitle: Every Rate, Threshold, and Calculation Your Business Needs for 2026 Compliance
html:
Getting payroll right in Jamaica means knowing the exact statutory rates and thresholds in force for the current year. For 2026, the core rates covering PAYE income tax, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax remain consistent with the framework established in recent years — but the details matter enormously. A single percentage point error compounded across a 50-person payroll over 12 months can produce significant under-remittance penalties from the Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ), NIS, or NHT. This guide gives you every number you need, with worked examples and rate tables you can put to use immediately.

Overview of Jamaica's Statutory Payroll Deductions

Every Jamaican employer operating on the formal payroll must administer four statutory deduction schemes. Two of these — PAYE and Education Tax — are remitted to TAJ. The other two — NIS and NHT — go to their respective statutory bodies. Each scheme has its own rate, ceiling, and filing deadline, and all four affect both the employee's net pay and the employer's cost of employment.

  • PAYE (Pay As You Earn) — income tax withheld from employees and remitted to TAJ
  • NIS (National Insurance Scheme) — social insurance contributions split between employee and employer
  • NHT (National Housing Trust) — housing fund contributions, also split
  • Education Tax — education levy on gross emoluments, split between employee and employer

Understanding how each interacts — particularly which earnings are included in the base, and where ceilings apply — is essential before running a single payslip.

PAYE Income Tax: 2026 Threshold and Rates

PAYE is the most complex of the four statutory deductions because it uses a progressive rate structure with an annual income tax threshold that shelters lower earners from income tax entirely.

The Annual Income Tax Threshold

For 2026, the annual income tax threshold in Jamaica is JMD $1,500,096. Any employee whose total annual emoluments fall at or below this figure pays zero PAYE. For employees earning above the threshold, only the portion exceeding JMD $1,500,096 is subject to tax.

On a monthly payroll, the monthly threshold is JMD $1,500,096 ÷ 12 = JMD $125,008. On a weekly payroll, the weekly threshold is JMD $1,500,096 ÷ 52 = JMD $28,848.

PAYE Tax Rates for 2026

Jamaica applies two income tax rates above the threshold:

  • 25% on chargeable income from JMD $1,500,097 up to JMD $6,000,000 per year
  • 30% on chargeable income exceeding JMD $6,000,000 per year

Chargeable income is calculated as: Gross Emoluments − Income Tax Threshold − Approved Deductions. Approved deductions include NIS contributions (employee portion), approved pension contributions, and certain other statutory allowances.

PAYE Rate Table for 2026

The table below summarises the tax bands applicable for annual earnings:

  • Annual income up to JMD $1,500,096: 0% (exempt)
  • Annual income JMD $1,500,097 to JMD $6,000,000: 25% on the amount above threshold
  • Annual income above JMD $6,000,000: 25% on the band JMD $1,500,097–$6,000,000, then 30% on the remainder

Worked PAYE Example — Monthly Salaried Employee

An employee earns a gross monthly salary of JMD $180,000. NIS employee contribution is JMD $5,400 (3% of $180,000).

  • Gross monthly emoluments: JMD $180,000
  • Less NIS employee contribution: JMD $5,400
  • Less monthly income tax threshold: JMD $125,008
  • Monthly chargeable income: JMD $49,592
  • PAYE at 25%: JMD $12,398
  • Net monthly take-home after all statutory deductions: approximately JMD $148,802 (before NHT and Education Tax deductions)

Worked PAYE Example — High Earner Above JMD $6 Million

An employee earns JMD $700,000 per month (JMD $8,400,000 annually). Annual chargeable income after threshold and NIS deductions:

  • Gross annual: JMD $8,400,000
  • Less annual threshold: JMD $1,500,096
  • Less annual NIS (3% up to $5M ceiling): JMD $150,000
  • Annual chargeable income: JMD $6,749,904
  • Tax on JMD $4,499,904 (the band $1,500,097–$6,000,000): 25% = JMD $1,124,976
  • Tax on JMD $749,904 (the amount above $6,000,000): 30% = JMD $224,971
  • Total annual PAYE: JMD $1,349,947 (approximately JMD $112,496/month)

NIS (National Insurance Scheme) Rates 2026

The National Insurance Scheme provides social protection coverage including sickness benefit, maternity benefit, and retirement pension. Both the employee and employer contribute.

NIS Rate for 2026

  • Employee contribution: 3% of insurable wage
  • Employer contribution: 3% of insurable wage
  • Total combined NIS contribution: 6%

NIS Earnings Ceiling

The NIS insurable wage ceiling for 2026 is JMD $5,000,000 per year, which equates to:

  • Monthly ceiling: JMD $416,667
  • Weekly ceiling: JMD $96,154
  • Fortnightly ceiling: JMD $192,308

Earnings above the ceiling are not subject to NIS contributions. This means the maximum NIS contribution per employee per year is JMD $150,000 each for employee and employer (3% × JMD $5,000,000).

Worked NIS Example

Employee earns JMD $250,000/month (below the monthly ceiling of JMD $416,667):

  • Employee NIS: JMD $250,000 × 3% = JMD $7,500
  • Employer NIS: JMD $250,000 × 3% = JMD $7,500

Employee earns JMD $600,000/month (above the monthly ceiling):

  • Contributions are capped at the ceiling: JMD $416,667 × 3% = JMD $12,500 each

NHT (National Housing Trust) Rates 2026

The National Housing Trust funds affordable housing for contributors who may apply for mortgage loans or draw-down benefits after qualifying contributions.

NHT Rate for 2026

  • Employee contribution: 2% of gross emoluments
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross emoluments
  • Total combined NHT contribution: 5%

NHT Earnings Ceiling

Unlike NIS, there is no earnings ceiling for NHT contributions. The 2% employee and 3% employer rates apply to the full gross emolument regardless of how high the salary is. This makes NHT particularly significant for high earners.

Worked NHT Example

Employee earns JMD $180,000/month:

  • Employee NHT: JMD $180,000 × 2% = JMD $3,600
  • Employer NHT: JMD $180,000 × 3% = JMD $5,400

Employee earns JMD $700,000/month:

  • Employee NHT: JMD $700,000 × 2% = JMD $14,000
  • Employer NHT: JMD $700,000 × 3% = JMD $21,000

Education Tax Rates 2026

Education Tax is a levy charged on the gross emoluments of employees, with separate rates for the employee and the employer. It is remitted to TAJ alongside PAYE.

Education Tax Rate for 2026

  • Employee contribution: 2.25% of gross emoluments
  • Employer contribution: 3.5% of gross emoluments

Education Tax Earnings Base

Education Tax applies to gross emoluments with no ceiling, similar to NHT. The base includes basic salary, overtime, bonuses, taxable allowances, and other employment income. It does not apply to the employer's NIS or NHT contributions themselves.

Worked Education Tax Example

Employee earns JMD $180,000/month:

  • Employee Education Tax: JMD $180,000 × 2.25% = JMD $4,050
  • Employer Education Tax: JMD $180,000 × 3.5% = JMD $6,300

Complete 2026 Statutory Rate Summary Table

For quick reference, here are all 2026 Jamaica payroll statutory rates in one place:

  • PAYE Threshold: JMD $1,500,096/year (zero tax below this level)
  • PAYE Rate Band 1: 25% on chargeable income JMD $1,500,097–$6,000,000/year
  • PAYE Rate Band 2: 30% on chargeable income above JMD $6,000,000/year
  • NIS Employee: 3% (ceiling JMD $5,000,000/year)
  • NIS Employer: 3% (ceiling JMD $5,000,000/year)
  • NHT Employee: 2% (no ceiling)
  • NHT Employer: 3% (no ceiling)
  • Education Tax Employee: 2.25% (no ceiling)
  • Education Tax Employer: 3.5% (no ceiling)

Total Employer Cost of Employment in 2026

Many employers focus only on what is deducted from the employee's payslip, but the employer also bears statutory costs on top of gross salary. For an employee on JMD $180,000/month:

  • Gross salary: JMD $180,000
  • Employer NIS (3%): JMD $5,400
  • Employer NHT (3%): JMD $5,400
  • Employer Education Tax (3.5%): JMD $6,300
  • Total employer monthly cost: JMD $197,100

This means the true employment cost is approximately 9.5% above the stated gross salary for employees well below the NIS ceiling. For high-earning employees above the NIS ceiling, the NIS employer cost is capped, reducing the premium slightly, but NHT and Education Tax continue without limit.

Remittance Deadlines and Filing Requirements

Knowing the rates is only half the battle — remitting on time is what keeps you compliant. The key deadlines for 2026 are:

  • PAYE and Education Tax: Must be remitted to TAJ by the 14th of the following month. Monthly returns (S01/Employer's Return) must accompany the payment.
  • NIS: Due to the National Insurance Fund by the 14th of the following month. Late payments attract interest and penalties.
  • NHT: Due to the National Housing Trust by the 14th of the following month. NHT also requires a monthly contribution schedule showing each employee's details.
  • Annual P45 returns: Must be filed with TAJ by March 31 of the following year, detailing each employee's annual emoluments and tax deducted.

Common Errors in Applying 2026 Rates

Even experienced payroll teams make these mistakes. Watch out for:

  • Applying NIS contributions above the ceiling: Once an employee's year-to-date insurable earnings reach JMD $5,000,000, no further NIS should be deducted for the remainder of the year. Failure to track this means over-deducting from employees.
  • Forgetting NHT has no ceiling: Unlike NIS, NHT continues on all earnings regardless of level. Many payroll systems configured with a ceiling for NIS incorrectly apply the same logic to NHT.
  • Not adjusting the PAYE threshold pro-rata for part-time employees: The threshold is based on annual income. A part-time employee working half the week still gets the full JMD $1,500,096 threshold applied against their annualised earnings — but you must annualise their actual part-time earnings to determine whether PAYE applies, not compare their weekly pay directly to the weekly threshold.
  • Mixing up PAYE and Education Tax base: PAYE is calculated on chargeable income (after threshold and approved deductions). Education Tax is calculated on gross emoluments — no threshold deduction applies.
  • Late remittance penalties: TAJ, NIS, and NHT all charge interest on late remittances. For TAJ, the rate is currently 16.5% per annum on the outstanding amount. Missing even one month's deadline can result in material penalty costs.

How Payroll Software Handles These Rates

Manually calculating PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax for each employee every pay period — correctly, with ceiling tracking, progressive rate banding, and threshold proration — is error-prone and time-consuming. Purpose-built Jamaica payroll software maintains all 2026 statutory rates internally, automatically applies the NIS ceiling on a year-to-date basis, generates the correct remittance schedules for TAJ/NIS/NHT, and produces the P45 annual return. This eliminates the most common class of payroll errors: incorrect rate application and missed ceiling adjustments.

Looking Ahead: Will Rates Change Mid-Year?

Jamaica's statutory rates are typically set at the start of the fiscal year (April) and the calendar year. The income tax threshold has historically been increased periodically by the Ministry of Finance through the annual budget exercise. Employers should monitor the annual Budget Presentation (typically February/March) for any threshold or rate changes taking effect from April 1. When changes occur, payroll systems and spreadsheets must be updated before the first payroll run of the new fiscal year — and year-to-date tax calculations may need adjustment to reflect the change retroactively to April 1 if the budget is not passed until later in the year.

Keeping your payroll system up to date with verified rates — rather than relying on memory or outdated guides — is the single most important compliance habit a Jamaican employer can develop.
